modules.title: Introduction to HTML

content.value: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating web pages. Think of HTML as the skeleton of a website - it provides the structure and foundation upon which everything else is built.
Imagine building a house. HTML is the framework - the walls, floors, and roof. CSS is the paint and decorations. JavaScript is the electricity and plumbing.
HTML was created by Tim Berners-Lee in 1991 at CERN. HTML5, released in 2014, is the current standard.

content.title: Basic HTML Document
content.code:
Hello, World!

This is my first HTML page.

content.value:

don't need a closing tag.

modules.title: HTML Document Structure

content.value: Every HTML document follows a specific structure: doctype, html, head, and body elements. This ensures consistent rendering across browsers.
An HTML document is like a book. The DOCTYPE tells the language. The head is the table of contents. The body is the actual story.

modules.title: Text Elements & Headings

content.value: HTML provides six heading levels from h1 to h6. Headings create a clear information hierarchy crucial for SEO and accessibility.
Headings work like newspaper sections. h1 is the main headline, h2 are section headers, h3 are article titles - each level gets more specific.

modules.title: Links & Navigation

content.value: The <a> tag creates hyperlinks for navigating pages, downloading files, or jumping to sections. Links connect the web together.
Links are like doorways. Each has a destination (URL). Some doors open in the same tab, others open in new tabs. The link text tells you where it goes.

modules.title: Lists & Tables

content.value: HTML provides three list types: unordered (<ul>) with bullets, ordered (<ol>) with numbers, and description lists (<dl>). Tables organize data in rows and columns.
Unordered lists are like grocery lists. Ordered lists are like recipes (sequence matters). Tables are like spreadsheets for easy comparison.

modules.title: Forms & Inputs

content.value: Forms collect user input. HTML provides many input types from text to date pickers. Labels and validation make forms accessible and user-friendly.
A form is like a paper application. Inputs are blanks to fill, labels tell you what to write, submit button hands it to the server.

content.title: Building Forms
content.code:
<form action="/submit" method="POST">
  <label for="name">Name:</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="name" required>
  <label for="email">Email:</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" required>
  <label for="password">Password:</label>
  <input type="password" id="password" minlength="8">
  <label><input type="checkbox"> Subscribe</label>
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

modules.title: SEO Best Practices

content.value: SEO makes websites more visible in search results. Clean semantic HTML with proper meta tags helps search engines understand and rank content.

content.title: SEO HTML
content.code:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <title>Learn HTML - Guide 2026</title>
  <meta name="description" content="HTML guide">
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://example.com/guide">
  <meta property="og:title" content="Learn HTML">
  <meta property="og:image" content="thumb.jpg">
  <script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https://schema.org","@type":"Article","headline":"Learn HTML"}</script>
</head>

modules.title: Flexbox

content.value: Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout model that distributes space and aligns items. Perfect for nav bars, cards, centering, and responsive grids.
Flexbox is like a luggage carousel. Items flow in one direction, can be centered, spaced evenly, or wrapped to the next row.

modules.title: CSS Grid

Flexbox is a strip (1D), Grid is a tray (2D). Grid splits space into cells, items can span multiple cells.

modules.title: File I/O & Error Handling

content.value: Python makes file I/O simple with open() and context managers (with). Exception handling uses try/except blocks.

content.title: File I/O
content.code:
with open('data.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()
    for line in file:
        print(line.strip())

with open('output.txt', 'w') as file:
    file.write('Hello, World!')

import json
with open('data.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

try:
    result = 10 / 0
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print('Cannot divide by zero')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Error: {e}')
finally:
    print('Cleanup runs always')

modules.title: SQL Introduction

content.value: SQL (Structured Query Language) manages relational databases. Used with MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc. Declarative language: you say WHAT, not HOW.
SQL is like a filing system. Tables are file cabinets, rows are individual files, columns are file labels. Queries search through everything.

modules.title: Queries & Filtering

content.value: SQL WHERE clause filters rows. Use operators: =, <>, <, >, LIKE (pattern), IN (list), BETWEEN (range), IS NULL.

content.title: Filtering
content.code:
SELECT * FROM products
WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 50
  AND category IN ('Electronics', 'Books')
  AND name LIKE '%phone%'
  AND description IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY price ASC
LIMIT 10;

SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products;

SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS count, AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY count DESC;

exercises: Filter products by price range
Use LIKE for name search
Group by category with COUNT

modules.title: Joins

content.value: Joins combine data from multiple tables using foreign keys. INNER JOIN shows matches only; LEFT JOIN keeps all left-table rows.
JOIN is like matching puzzle pieces. INNER JOIN only keeps where pieces fit. LEFT JOIN keeps all pieces from one side and fills gaps with null.

content.title: Joins
content.code:
SELECT o.id, u.name, o.total
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id;

SELECT u.name, o.id AS order_id
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

SELECT u.name, p.title AS product, oi.quantity
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.id;

explanation.desc: All left rows, null for no match

exercises: Join orders with users
Left join showing all customers

modules.title: Git Introduction
objectives: Understand what Git is and version control

content.value: Git is a distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds in 2005. It tracks changes in files, enables collaboration, and maintains a complete history of your project.
Git is like a time machine for your code. You can save snapshots (commits), go back in time (revert), create alternate timelines (branches), and merge them back together.

content.title: Getting Started with Git
content.code:
# Install Git: https://git-scm.com
# Configure your identity
git config --global user.name 'Your Name'
git config --global user.email 'your@email.com'

# Check configuration
git config --list

# Initialize a new repository
mkdir my-project
cd my-project
git init

# Check status
git status

# Add files to staging area
echo '# My Project' > README.md
git add README.md
git add .  # Add all files

# Commit staged changes
git commit -m 'Initial commit: add README'

# View commit history
git log
git log --oneline  # Compact view

explanation.desc: Shows working tree and staging state
Saves staged changes with a message

exercises: Install Git and configure your name/email
Initialize a repository and check status
Stage and commit a new file

modules.title: Working with Commits
objectives: Use git diff to review changes

content.value: Commits are the building blocks of Git history. Each commit is a snapshot of your project at a point in time. Good commit messages follow conventions like 'type: description'.

content.title: Working with Commits
content.code:
# View changes before staging
git diff

# View staged changes
git diff --staged

# Commit with detailed message
git commit -m 'feat: add user authentication

- Implement login page
- Add password hashing
- Create session management'

# Amend last commit (don't amend pushed commits!)
git add forgotten-file.js
git commit --amend -m 'fix: correct typo in header'

# Revert a commit (safe for shared history)
git revert HEAD

# Reset (be careful!)
git reset HEAD~1  # Remove last commit, keep changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1  # Remove last commit and changes

explanation.desc: Shows unstaged changes line by line
Creates new commit that undoes previous
Moves branch pointer back one commit

exercises: Make a commit with a detailed message
Amend a commit to add a forgotten file
Practice git diff to review changes

modules.title: Branching & Merging

content.value: Branches let you work on features independently. The main branch (usually main or master) should remain stable. Feature branches isolate work until it is ready to merge.
Branches are like alternate universes. You create a new universe to experiment safely. When your experiment works, you merge it back to the main timeline. If it fails, you just delete that universe.

content.title: Branching & Merging
content.code:
# List branches
git branch
git branch -a  # All branches including remote

# Create and switch
git branch feature-login
git checkout feature-login
# Shorthand:
git checkout -b feature-signup

# Merge a branch
git checkout main
git merge feature-login

# Fast-forward merge (linear history)
git merge --ff-only feature

# Merge with explicit commit
git merge --no-ff feature -m 'merge: add login feature'

# Resolve conflicts
# 1. Git marks conflicted files
# 2. Edit files to resolve conflicts
# 3. git add resolved-files
# 4. git commit

# Delete a branch
git branch -d feature-login  # Safe delete
git branch -D feature-login  # Force delete (unmerged)

explanation.desc: Create and switch to new branch in one command
Creates merge commit even if fast-forward possible
Git shows both versions in conflicted files

exercises: Create a new branch and switch to it
Make changes and merge back to main
Create a merge conflict and resolve it

modules.title: Remote Repositories & GitHub

content.value: Remote repositories (like GitHub) host your Git repos online. They enable collaboration, backup, and continuous integration. The default remote is named 'origin'.

content.title: Working with Remotes
content.code:
# Add a remote
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo.git

# List remotes
git remote -v

# Push to remote
git push origin main

# Set upstream and push
git push -u origin main  # -u sets upstream tracking
# Next time: git push (works without args)

# Pull latest changes
git pull origin main

# Fetch without merging
git fetch origin

# View remote branches
git branch -r

# Clone a repository
git clone https://github.com/username/repo.git

# Push a new branch to remote
git push -u origin feature-login

# Delete remote branch
git push origin --delete feature-login

explanation.desc: Link local repo to remote GitHub
Push to remote and set tracking reference

exercises: Create a GitHub repository and push code
Clone a repository from GitHub
Pull changes after a teammate pushes

modules.title: Undoing Changes
objectives: Use git reset vs git revert
Use git stash for temporary work

content.value: Git provides several ways to undo changes at different levels. Understanding when to use reset, revert, restore, and stash is crucial for safe version control.

content.title: Undoing Changes
content.code:
# Unstage a file (keep changes)
git reset HEAD file.js
# Modern alternative:
git restore --staged file.js

# Discard working directory changes
git restore file.js
# Or: git checkout -- file.js

# Undo all unstaged changes
git restore .

# Stash temporary work
git stash -u  # Stash including untracked files
git stash list
git stash pop  # Apply and remove stash
git stash apply stash@{0}  # Apply without removing

# Recover a dropped commit
git reflog
# Find the commit hash, then:
git cherry-pick <hash>
git checkout -b recovered-branch <hash>

# Interactive rebase (edit history)
git rebase -i HEAD~3  # Rewrite last 3 commits

explanation.desc: Save work in progress with untracked files
Apply most recent stash and remove it
Reference log - records all HEAD movements
Interactive rebase to edit commit history

exercises: Stage a file then unstage it with restore
Stash changes and apply them later
Use reflog to recover a lost commit

modules.title: GitHub Collaboration

content.value: Pull Requests (PRs) are the heart of GitHub collaboration. You fork a repo, make changes, and submit a PR for review. Code reviews ensure quality and share knowledge across teams.

content.title: GitHub Collaboration Workflow
content.code:
# Fork on GitHub first

# Clone your fork
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/project.git
cd project

# Add upstream remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/ORIGINAL_OWNER/project.git

# Create feature branch
git checkout -b feature/amazing-feature

# Make changes, commit, push
git add .
git commit -m 'feat: add amazing feature'
git push -u origin feature/amazing-feature

# Create PR on GitHub

# Sync fork with upstream
git fetch upstream
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main
git push origin main

# Update PR branch
git checkout feature/amazing-feature
git merge main  # Or git rebase main

# Squash commits in PR
git rebase -i main
# Then force push: git push --force-with-lease

explanation.desc: Creates your personal copy of a repository
Link original repo as upstream remote
Propose changes from your fork to original
Team members review and comment on changes
Safer force push that checks for remote changes

exercises: Fork a repository and clone it locally
Create a pull request with changes
Sync your fork with the upstream repository

modules.title: Git Best Practices & Workflows
objectives: Write meaningful commit messages with conventional commits

content.value: Git workflows define how teams use branches. GitFlow uses develop/feature/release/hotfix branches. Trunk-based development uses short-lived feature branches merging to main frequently.

content.title: Best Practices
content.code:
# .gitignore example
node_modules/
.env
*.log
dist/
build/
.DS_Store

# Conventional commits
# feat: new feature
# fix: bug fix
# docs: documentation only
# style: formatting, missing semi-colons
# refactor: code restructuring
# test: adding tests
# chore: build tasks, package manager

# Example messages
git commit -m 'feat(auth): implement OAuth2 login'
git commit -m 'fix(api): correct pagination offset'
git commit -m 'docs(README): update installation guide'

# Protect main branch (GitHub settings)
# - Require PR reviews
# - Require status checks
# - Require up-to-date branches
# - Include administrators

# Signed commits for verification
git commit -S -m 'feat: add verified commit'

# Always pull before pushing
git pull --rebase  # Rebases your commits on top
git push

explanation.desc: Patterns for files Git should ignore
Prevent direct pushes to important branches
Sign commit with GPG key for verification
Rebase local commits on top of remote changes

exercises: Create a comprehensive .gitignore for a Node project
Write 5 conventional commits for different types
Set up branch protection rules on GitHub

modules.title: Project: Collaboration Simulation
objectives: Simulate real team collaboration with Git

content.value: Practice full collaboration workflow: two developers work on separate features, create branches, submit PRs, handle reviews, and resolve merge conflicts.

content.title: Collaboration Project
content.code:
# === Person A: Initialize project ===
mkdir collab-project && cd collab-project
git init
echo '# Collab Project' > README.md
echo 'node_modules/' > .gitignore
git add .
git commit -m 'chore: initial project setup'

# === Person B: Clone and create feature ===
git clone <repo-url> collab-project
cd collab-project
git checkout -b feature/user-auth

# Add login functionality
echo 'function login() { return true; }' > auth.js
git add auth.js
git commit -m 'feat(auth): add login function'
git push -u origin feature/user-auth
# Create PR on GitHub

# === Person A: Review and merge PR ===
# Review PR, leave comments, approve
# Merge via GitHub or locally:
git checkout main
git pull
git merge --no-ff feature/user-auth
git push

# === Conflict scenario ===
# Both edit same lines
# Person A:
echo 'color: blue' > styles.css
git add .; git commit -m 'style: change to blue'

# Person B (after Person A pushed):
echo 'color: red' > styles.css
git add .; git commit -m 'style: change to red'
git push  # Rejected!

# Person B resolves:
git pull
# Conflict in styles.css - resolve it
# Then:
git add styles.css
git commit -m 'fix: resolve color conflict'
git push

explanation.desc: Each feature gets its own branch
Remote has commits you don't have
Brings remote changes and shows conflicts
Edit file, remove markers, add, commit

Create a pull request and have it reviewed

modules.title: MongoDB Introduction

content.value: MongoDB is a NoSQL document database that stores data in flexible, JSON-like documents. Unlike SQL tables with fixed schemas, MongoDB collections can have documents with different fields.

If SQL databases are like spreadsheets with strict columns, MongoDB is like a filing cabinet where each file (document) can have its own structure. Some files have extra pages, others don't - and that's okay!

content.title: MongoDB Basics

explanation.desc: JSON-like data with embedded objects and arrays
Each document can have different fields
Query all documents with formatted output

objectives: Perform Create, Read, Update, Delete operations

modules.title: Schema Design & Data Modeling

content.value: MongoDB schema design differs from SQL. You can embed related data in a single document (denormalization) or reference documents across collections (normalization). Choose based on access patterns.

Embedding is like putting all related info in one folder. Referencing is like having a filing system where folders point to each other. Embedding is faster for reads, referencing is better when data grows independently.

content.title: Schema Design Patterns

explanation.desc: Store related data within parent document
Store references to documents in other collections
Index on multiple fields for complex queries
Ensures no duplicate values for indexed field

modules.title: Replication & Sharding

content.value: Replication provides data redundancy across servers. A replica set has one primary and multiple secondaries. Sharding distributes data across multiple machines (shards) for horizontal scaling.

content.title: Replica Set & Sharding
content.code:
# Replica Set Configuration
# Start three mongod instances:
mongod --replSet rs0 --port 27017 --dbpath /data/rs1
mongod --replSet rs0 --port 27018 --dbpath /data/rs2
mongod --replSet rs0 --port 27019 --dbpath /data/rs3

# In mongosh, initiate replica set:
rs.initiate({
  _id: 'rs0',
  members: [
    { _id: 0, host: 'localhost:27017' },
    { _id: 1, host: 'localhost:27018' },
    { _id: 2, host: 'localhost:27019' }
  ]
})

# Check status
rs.status()

# Read from secondary (eventually consistent)
db.getMongo().setReadPref('secondary')

# Sharding Setup
# Start config servers and mongos, then:
sh.enableSharding('myDatabase')

# Choose shard key (crucial!)
db.users.createIndex({ userId: 1 })
sh.shardCollection('myDatabase.users', { userId: 1 })

# Hash-based sharding for even distribution
sh.shardCollection('myDatabase.logs', { _id: 'hashed' })

# Check sharding status
sh.status()

explanation.desc: Primary handles writes, secondaries replicate data
Allows read scaling with eventual consistency
Field used to distribute documents across shards
Even distribution using hash of shard key

exercises: Set up a 3-node replica set locally
Configure read preference to secondary
Enable sharding on a collection with a shard key

modules.title: Docker Introduction
objectives: Understand what Docker is and containers vs VMs

content.value: Docker is a platform for developing, shipping, and running applications in containers. Containers package code and dependencies together, ensuring it runs the same everywhere.

Containers are like shipping containers for software. A shipping container holds cargo securely and stacks on any ship, truck, or train. Docker containers hold your app and run on any system with Docker installed.

content.title: Getting Started with Docker
content.code:
# Verify installation
docker --version
docker info

# Run first container
docker run hello-world

# Run nginx web server
docker run -d -p 8080:80 --name my-nginx nginx
# -d: detached mode
# -p: port mapping (host:container)
# --name: container name

# List containers
docker ps          # Running
docker ps -a       # All containers

# View logs
docker logs my-nginx

# Execute command in running container
docker exec -it my-nginx bash

# Stop and remove
docker stop my-nginx
docker rm my-nginx

# List images
docker images

# Remove image
docker rmi nginx

explanation.desc: Pulls and runs a test container
Map host port 8080 to container port 80

exercises: Install Docker and run hello-world
Run an nginx container and access it on port 8080
Execute bash inside running container

quiz.question: Docker containers share what with host?

modules.title: Docker Images & Dockerfile
objectives: Create custom Docker images with Dockerfile

content.value: A Dockerfile is a script with instructions to build a Docker image. Each instruction creates a layer. Docker caches layers to speed up builds. Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files.

content.title: Dockerfile Examples
content.code:
# Node.js app Dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /app

# Copy package files first (leverage caching)
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Copy and build source
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Production stage
FROM node:18-alpine

WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules

EXPOSE 3000

USER node

CMD ['node', 'dist/server.js']

# .dockerignore
node_modules
.git
*.md
.env

# Build commands
docker build -t my-app:latest .
docker tag my-app:latest my-app:v1.0
docker image ls

# Build with specific Dockerfile
docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t my-app:prod .

explanation.desc: Builder stage compiles, final stage runs

exercises: Create a Dockerfile for a Node.js app
Use multi-stage build for smaller image
Build and tag the image with a version

modules.title: Docker Compose

content.value: Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container Docker applications. A compose file specifies services, networks, and volumes. One command starts everything.

content.title: Docker Compose
content.code:
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'

services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
      - DB_HOST=db
    depends_on:
      - db
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - /app/node_modules

  db:
    image: postgres:15-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    ports:
      - '5432:5432'

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - '6379:6379'

volumes:
  pgdata:

# Commands
docker compose up -d          # Start all services
docker compose down           # Stop and remove
docker compose logs -f        # Follow logs
docker compose ps             # List service containers
docker compose exec web bash  # Execute in service
docker compose build          # Rebuild images
docker compose -p myproject   # Set project name

# Profiles (start specific services)
docker compose --profile dev up

explanation.desc: Bind mount for live code reload

exercises: Create a compose file with web and database services
Use volumes for data persistence
Start services with docker compose up

modules.title: Networks & Volumes
objectives: Manage bind mounts and tmpfs mounts

content.value: Docker networks enable container communication. Bridge networks isolate containers. Volumes persist data independent of container lifecycle. Bind mounts share host directories.

content.title: Networks & Volumes
content.code:
# Network Commands
docker network ls
docker network create my-network
docker network connect my-network my-container
docker network inspect my-network

# Run container with custom network
docker run -d --network my-network --name app nginx

# Volume Commands
docker volume ls
docker volume create my-data

# Run with named volume
docker run -d -v my-data:/data nginx

# Bind mount (host directory)
docker run -d -v C:/my-project:/app nginx

# Read-only bind mount
docker run -d -v C:/config:/etc/nginx/conf.d:ro nginx

# tmpfs (temporary in-memory)
docker run --tmpfs /tmp:size=100M nginx

# Backup a volume
docker run --rm -v my-data:/source -v C:/backup:/dest alpine tar czf /dest/backup.tar.gz -C /source .

# Restore a volume
docker run --rm -v my-data:/dest -v C:/backup:/source alpine tar xzf /source/backup.tar.gz -C /dest

explanation.desc: Mount named volume to container path

exercises: Create a custom bridge network and connect two containers
Create a named volume and persist data
Use bind mount for development code sharing

modules.title: Docker Security

content.value: Container security involves multiple layers: base image choice, running as non-root, read-only filesystems, secrets management, and vulnerability scanning.

content.title: Docker Security
content.code:
# Dockerfile Security Best Practices
FROM node:18-alpine

# Create non-root user
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup

# Set secure defaults
WORKDIR /app

COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup package*.json ./

RUN npm ci --only=production

COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .

# Use non-root user
USER appuser

# Read-only root filesystem (in compose: read_only: true)

# HEALTHCHECK instruction
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

# Scan for vulnerabilities
docker scout quickstart
docker scout cves my-image:latest

# Third-party scanners
docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock aquasec/trivy image my-image:latest

# Security options at runtime
docker run \
  --read-only \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges:true \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  my-app

explanation.desc: Drop all Linux capabilities, add only needed

exercises: Create a Dockerfile with non-root user
Scan an image for vulnerabilities with Trivy
Run a container with read-only filesystem

modules.title: Docker Registries & CI/CD
objectives: Push and pull images from Docker Hub

content.value: Docker registries store and distribute container images. Docker Hub is the default public registry. CI/CD pipelines build, test, and push images automatically on each commit.

content.title: Registries & CI/CD
content.code:
# Push to Docker Hub
docker login -u username
docker tag my-app username/my-app:v1.0.0
docker push username/my-app:v1.0.0

# Pull and run
docker pull username/my-app:v1.0.0
docker run -d -p 3000:3000 username/my-app:v1.0.0

# GitHub Actions CI/CD for Docker
# .github/workflows/docker.yml
name: Build and Push Docker Image
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Login to Docker Hub
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          username: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_USERNAME }}
          password: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_PASSWORD }}
      
      - name: Build and push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          push: true
          tags: |
            username/my-app:latest
            username/my-app:${{ github.sha }}
      
      - name: Run Trivy scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: 'username/my-app:latest'
          format: 'table'
          exit-code: '1'

explanation.desc: Authenticate and transfer images to/from registry
Automated build pipeline on git push
GitHub Action to authenticate with registry

exercises: Push an image to Docker Hub
Create a GitHub Actions workflow for Docker
Tag images with semantic versioning

modules.title: Docker in Production

content.value: Production Docker requires resource management, monitoring, logging, and orchestration. Docker Swarm is built-in orchestration. Kubernetes is the industry standard for large deployments.

content.title: Production Docker
content.code:
# Resource limits
docker run -d --name app \
  --memory='512m' \
  --cpus='0.5' \
  --restart=unless-stopped \
  nginx

# Update resource limits
docker update --memory='1g' --cpus='1' app

# Docker Swarm Init
docker swarm init --advertise-addr 192.168.1.100
docker swarm join-token worker

# Deploy stack
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml myapp

docker service ls
docker service ps myapp_web

# Container monitoring
docker stats
docker stats --no-stream --format 'table {{.Name}}	{{.CPUPerc}}	{{.MemUsage}}'

# View logs
docker logs --tail 100 -f app

# Logging driver
docker run --log-driver json-file --log-opt max-size=10m --log-opt max-file=3 nginx

# Clean up unused resources
docker system df
docker system prune -a --volumes

# Health check in compose
docker inspect --format='{{json .State.Health}}' app

exercises: Run a container with memory and CPU limits
Initialize Docker Swarm and deploy a stack
Monitor containers with docker stats

modules.title: Project: Full Stack App with Docker
objectives: Use Docker Compose for multi-service setup
Set up development and production configurations

content.value: Containerize a full stack app with React frontend, Node.js backend, MongoDB database, and Nginx reverse proxy. Use multi-stage builds and Docker Compose for orchestration.

content.title: Full Stack Docker Setup
content.code:
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'

services:
  frontend:
    build:
      context: ./frontend
      target: production
    ports:
      - '80:80'
    depends_on:
      - api

  api:
    build:
      context: ./api
      target: production
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
      - MONGODB_URI=mongodb://db:27017/myapp
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped

  db:
    image: mongo:7
    volumes:
      - mongo-data:/data/db
    healthcheck:
      test: echo 'db.runCommand("ping").ok' | mongosh --quiet
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  mongo-data:

# Frontend Dockerfile (multi-stage)
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:18 AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Serve with Nginx
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 80
CMD ['nginx', '-g', 'daemon off;']

# API Dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
COPY . .
USER node
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ['node', 'server.js']

# .dockerignore for each service
node_modules
.git
.env
*.md
tests/
coverage/

explanation.desc: Build in node image, serve from nginx
Wait for database health check before starting API
Define how Docker checks service health
Efficient static file serving from nginx

exercises: Create Dockerfiles for frontend and backend services
Write docker-compose.yml with health checks
Run the full stack with docker compose up

modules.title: Linux Introduction & Installation

content.value: Linux is a free, open-source Unix-like operating system kernel created by Linus Torvalds in 1991. Distributions (distros) like Ubuntu, Fedora, and Debian package the kernel with software for different use cases.
The Linux kernel is like a car engine. Distributions are like car models - same engine but different features. Ubuntu is a family sedan, Arch is a race car, and Debian is a reliable truck.

content.title: Getting Started with Linux
content.code:
# Check Linux version
cat /etc/os-release
uname -a

# File system navigation
pwd                    # Print working directory
ls -la                 # List all files with details
cd /home/user          # Change directory
cd ~                   # Go home
cd ..                  # Up one level

# View system info
top                    # Running processes
free -h                # Memory usage
df -h                  # Disk usage
whoami                 # Current user
id                     # User and group IDs

# Getting help
man ls                 # Manual for ls command
ls --help              # Built-in help
tldr ls                # Simplified man pages (if installed)

# Shutdown/restart
sudo shutdown -h now   # Shutdown immediately
sudo reboot            # Reboot

explanation.desc: List with hidden files (-a) and details (-l)

exercises: Install Linux via WSL or VM
Navigate the file system with cd and ls
Use man to learn about 3 new commands

modules.title: File System & Permissions

content.value: Linux uses a hierarchical file system rooted at /. Standard directories: /bin (binaries), /etc (configs), /home (users), /var (variable data), /tmp (temporary). Everything is a file.

content.title: File System & Permissions
content.code:
# File operations
touch file.txt          # Create empty file
mkdir -p dir/subdir     # Create directory (with parents)
cp source dest          # Copy file
cp -r dir1 dir2         # Copy directory recursively
mv old new              # Move/rename
rm file.txt             # Remove file
rm -rf dir              # Remove directory (dangerous!)

# View files
cat file.txt            # Display entire file
less file.txt           # Scroll through file
head -n 5 file.txt      # First 5 lines
tail -f file.txt        # Follow file (like logs)

# Permissions
chmod 755 script.sh     # rwxr-xr-x
chmod u+x file          # Add execute for user
chown user:group file   # Change owner/group

# Wildcards
ls *.txt                # All .txt files
ls file??.txt           # file followed by 2 chars
ls [abc]*               # Starts with a, b, or c

# Redirection
echo 'text' > file.txt  # Overwrite
echo 'more' >> file.txt # Append
ls nonexist 2> error.log # Redirect stderr
cat < file.txt          # Input redirection

# Pipes
ls -la | grep '.txt'    # Filter output
ps aux | sort -nk 3     # Sort by CPU
ps aux | wc -l          # Count lines

explanation.desc: Owner: rwx, Group: rx, Others: rx
Pass command output to next command

exercises: Create directory structure for a project
Set execute permission on a script
Use pipes to filter and count files

modules.title: Package Management & Software
objectives: Use apt, yum, and dnf package managers

content.value: Package managers handle software installation, updates, and dependencies. Debian/Ubuntu use apt (apt-get). Red Hat/Fedora use dnf (formerly yum). Arch uses pacman.

content.title: Package Management
content.code:
# Debian/Ubuntu (apt)
sudo apt update                    # Update package list
sudo apt upgrade                   # Upgrade all packages
sudo apt install nginx             # Install package
sudo apt remove nginx              # Remove package
sudo apt purge nginx               # Remove package + configs
sudo apt autoremove                # Remove unused dependencies

# Search packages
apt search 'web server'
apt show nginx

# Red Hat/Fedora (dnf)
sudo dnf install nginx
sudo dnf update
sudo dnf remove nginx

# Snap packages
sudo snap install --classic code
sudo snap list

# Flatpak
flatpak install flathub org.gimp.GIMP

# Compile from source
tar -xzf program.tar.gz
cd program
./configure --prefix=/usr/local
make
sudo make install

# Check installed packages
dpkg -l | grep nginx   # Debian
rpm -qa | grep nginx   # Red Hat

explanation.code: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade
explanation.desc: Refresh package list then upgrade all
Search repositories and view package info

exercises: Use apt to install a web server
Search for a package and view its details
Install and remove a Snap package

modules.title: Process Management & System Monitoring
objectives: Manage processes with ps, top, kill

content.value: Linux manages processes with PIDs. systemd is the init system for modern distros. Use systemctl to control services. cron schedules recurring tasks.

content.title: Process Management & Systemd
content.code:
# Process management
ps aux                    # All running processes
ps aux | grep nginx       # Find specific process
top                       # Interactive process viewer
htop                      # Better top (if installed)

# Kill processes
kill PID                  # Graceful termination
kill -9 PID               # Force kill (SIGKILL)
k illall nginx             # Kill all by name

# Background/Foreground
command &                 # Run in background
Ctrl+Z                    # Suspend process
bg                        # Resume in background
fg                        # Bring to foreground
jobs                      # List background jobs

# Systemd service management
sudo systemctl start nginx
sudo systemctl stop nginx
sudo systemctl restart nginx
sudo systemctl status nginx
sudo systemctl enable nginx   # Start on boot
sudo systemctl disable nginx

# View logs
journalctl -u nginx            # Service logs
dmesg | tail                   # Kernel messages

# Cron jobs
crontab -e                     # Edit cron table
# Format: minute hour day month weekday command
# Example: Run backup at 2 AM daily
0 2 * * * /home/user/backup.sh

# At (one-time scheduled task)
echo 'shutdown -h now' | at 23:00

explanation.code: crontab format: m h d mon w cmd

exercises: Use ps aux to find a process and kill it

modules.title: Shell Scripting
objectives: Write Bash scripts with variables and control flow

content.value: Bash (Bourne Again SHell) scripting automates repetitive tasks. Scripts are text files with commands, variables, conditionals, and loops. Start with #!/bin/bash shebang.

Shell scripting is like writing a recipe. Each command is a step. Variables are like ingredient measurements. Functions are like sub-recipes you reuse. Loops are like doing the same step for each ingredient.

content.title: Shell Scripting
content.code:
#!/bin/bash

# Variables
NAME='World'
echo "Hello, $NAME!"

# Command substitution
DATE=$(date +%F)
echo "Today is $DATE"

# Arguments
echo "Script: $0"
echo "First arg: $1"
echo "All args: $@"
echo "Arg count: $#"

# Conditionals
if [ -f 'file.txt' ]; then
  echo 'File exists'
elif [ -d 'dir' ]; then
  echo 'Directory exists'
else
  echo 'Neither exists'
fi

# Loops
for file in *.txt; do
  echo "Processing $file"
  wc -l "$file"
done

# While loop with counter
count=1
while [ $count -le 5 ]; do
  echo "Count: $count"
  ((count++))
done

# Functions
backup_file() {
  local file="$1"
  cp "$file" "$file.bak"
  echo "Backup of $file created"
}

backup_file 'config.json'

# Error handling
set -e  # Exit on error
trap 'echo "Error on line $LINENO"' ERR

# Read file line by line
while IFS= read -r line; do
  echo "Line: $line"
done < 'input.txt'

explanation.desc: Shebang tells the system which interpreter to use
Check if file exists (-f) or directory (-d)
Loop over matching files with glob
Exit on error and error handler

exercises: Write a script that backs up a directory

modules.title: Networking & Security

content.value: Linux networking includes interfaces, routing, DNS, and firewalls. SSH provides secure remote access. ufw (Uncomplicated Firewall) makes iptables management easier. Security involves user management, SSH keys, and regular updates.

content.title: Networking & Security
content.code:
# Network configuration
ip addr                   # Show IP addresses
ip link                   # Show interfaces
ip route                  # Show routing table
ping -c 4 google.com      # Test connectivity

# DNS and network info
nslookup example.com      # DNS lookup
dig example.com           # Detailed DNS
curl ifconfig.me          # Get public IP
ss -tulpn                 # Listening ports and services

# SSH
ssh user@192.168.1.100    # Connect to remote
ssh-keygen -t ed25519     # Generate SSH key pair
ssh-copy-id user@host     # Copy public key to server

# SSH config (~/.ssh/config)
# Host myserver
#   HostName 192.168.1.100
#   User john
#   Port 2222
#   IdentityFile ~/.ssh/server_ed25519

# Firewall (ufw)
sudo ufw status verbose
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw allow ssh
sudo ufw allow 80/tcp
sudo ufw allow 443/tcp
sudo ufw enable

# Security hardening
sudo passwd -l root                  # Lock root password
sudo usermod -aG sudo username       # Grant sudo access
grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log  # Check failed logins

# Fail2ban for brute force protection
sudo apt install fail2ban
sudo systemctl enable fail2ban

# Regular updates
sudo unattended-upgrades             # Auto security updates
sudo apt install --only-upgrade openssl  # Update specific package

explanation.desc: Install public key on remote server
Block all incoming traffic by default
Brute force protection by banning IPs

exercises: Set up SSH key authentication
Configure ufw to allow only SSH and HTTP
Install and enable fail2ban

modules.title: Kotlin Basics
objectives: Install Kotlin and set up a project
Write your first Hello World program
Learn basic syntax and entry point

content.value: Kotlin is a modern, statically-typed language that runs on the JVM, developed by JetBrains. It is fully interoperable with Java, concise, and designed to be safe and expressive. Kotlin is the official language for Android development.

Kotlin is like Java with superpowers. If Java is a reliable sedan, Kotlin is the same car but with a turbo engine, better steering, and an autopilot - it compiles to the same bytecode but with much less ceremony.

content.title: Hello World

explanation.desc: Entry point of every Kotlin program

content.value: Kotlin source files use the .kt extension. Compile with kotlinc or use an IDE like IntelliJ IDEA. The fun keyword declares functions, and val declares read-only variables.

exercises: Write a program that prints your name and age
Create a program that takes user input and prints a greeting
Experiment with different print statements and string templates

quiz.question: What keyword declares a function in Kotlin?
Which is the correct entry point?
What file extension does Kotlin use?

modules.title: Variables & Types
objectives: Master basic data types (Int, Double, String, Boolean)
Work with nullable and non-nullable types

content.value: Kotlin has two variable keywords: val (immutable, read-only) and var (mutable). Type inference means the compiler deduces the type from the assigned value. Basic types include Int, Long, Double, Float, String, Boolean, and Char.

Think of val as a signed contract - once you sign, you cannot change it. var is like a whiteboard - you can erase and rewrite. Type inference is like having an assistant who labels boxes based on what you put inside.

content.title: Variables & Types
content.code:
val name = "Alice"
var age = 25
age = 26

val pi: Double = 3.14159
val isActive = true
val letter = 'A'
val bigNumber = 100L
val binary = 0b1010
val hex = 0xFF

println("$name is $age years old")
println("Type: ${name::class.simpleName}")

explanation.desc: Immutable string variable with type inference

content.value: Kotlin does not have primitive types - everything is an object. Types like Int, Double are compiled to JVM primitives for performance. Use underscore in large numbers for readability: val million = 1_000_000.

exercises: Create variables for a person: name, age, height, isStudent using both val and var
Experiment with type inference by assigning different values and checking their types
Use underscores in numeric literals for readability

quiz.question: Which keyword creates an immutable variable?

modules.title: Null Safety

content.value: Null safety is one of Kotlin's signature features. Types are non-nullable by default. To allow null, append ? to the type. This eliminates NullPointerException at compile time.

Nullable types are like a box that might be empty. The ?. operator is like carefully peeking inside. The Elvis ?: provides a default if empty. !! is like ripping the box open - use only if you are 100% sure it is full.

content.title: Null Safety
content.code:
var name: String? = null
println(name?.length)
println(name?.length ?: "Empty")

name = "Kotlin"
println(name!!.length)

val len = name?.length ?: return

// Safe casts
val obj: Any = "Hello"
val str: String? = obj as? String

// let with safe call
name?.let { println("Not null: $it") }

explanation.desc: Safe call - returns null if receiver is null
Safe cast - returns null if cast fails

content.value: The let function combined with safe call is a powerful pattern for working with nullable values. Smart casts automatically cast after null checks, so once you check name != null, the compiler treats it as non-nullable within that scope.

exercises: Write a program that safely prints the length of a nullable string
Use Elvis operator to provide default values for nullable fields
Demonstrate smart casts after null checks

quiz.question: How to declare a nullable String?

modules.title: Control Flow

content.value: Kotlin transforms traditional control flow. if can return a value (no ternary). when replaces switch with powerful pattern matching. Ranges with .. make loops elegant.

if as an expression is like a fork in the road that gives you a souvenir - whichever path you take, you get a result. when is a deluxe vending machine - press any button, and it finds the right snack based on what you pressed.

content.title: Control Flow

explanation.desc: if as expression returning a value

content.value: Ranges are expressions. Use in and !in to check membership. when can be used without an argument as a cleaner if-else chain. when branches can check types, ranges, or arbitrary conditions.

exercises: Write a program that uses if as an expression to determine pass/fail
Use when to map numeric grades to letter grades
Print even numbers from 0 to 20 using ranges

quiz.question: Does Kotlin have a ternary operator?
Which loop runs at least once?

modules.title: Functions & Lambdas

content.value: Functions are first-class citizens in Kotlin. They support default arguments, named parameters, single-expression syntax, and lambdas with concise syntax. Higher-order functions take or return functions.

Functions in Kotlin are like LEGO instructions - they can have default steps (parameters), be named clearly, and even be passed around as if they were physical pieces. Lambdas are like inline instruction sets you create on the fly.

content.title: Functions & Lambdas

explanation.code: fun add(a: Int, b: Int) = a + b

content.value: When a lambda is the last parameter, you can move it outside the parentheses (trailing lambda syntax). The implicit it name is available for single-parameter lambdas. Use _ for unused parameters.

exercises: Write a function with default parameters and call it with named arguments
Create a higher-order function that applies a transformation to a list
Use map and filter with lambda syntax on a list of numbers

quiz.question: Trailing lambda syntax means?
options: Lambda outside parentheses if last param

quiz.question: What is the implicit lambda parameter?

modules.title: Classes & Objects

content.value: Kotlin classes are concise. The primary constructor is part of the class header. init blocks run during instantiation. Properties combine fields with auto-generated accessors.

A Kotlin class is like a house blueprint. The primary constructor declares the lot size (parameters). init blocks are the foundation work done before you move in. Properties are rooms with automatic doors (getters/setters).

content.title: Classes & Objects

explanation.code: class Person(val name: String, var age: Int)

content.value: The primary constructor cannot contain code - use init blocks instead. Backing fields (field) are auto-generated for properties that need storage. Use _name convention for property backing if you want custom logic.

exercises: Create a Car class with make, model, year properties and a custom getter for age
Add an init block that validates year is not in the future
Create a custom setter that capitalizes the model name

quiz.question: Where does primary constructor code go?

modules.title: Inheritance & Interfaces

content.value: Classes are final by default in Kotlin. Use open to allow inheritance. Override must be explicit with the override keyword. Interfaces can have abstract and default methods.

Inheritance in Kotlin is like a locked door - you need a key (open) to open it. Override is like putting a sign on the door saying "my version". Interfaces are like USB ports - any device implementing the standard can plug in.

content.title: Inheritance & Interfaces

explanation.desc: Allows inheritance (classes are final by default)

content.value: Use abstract classes for shared state and behavior. Interfaces are for contracts (capabilities). A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one class. The override modifier is mandatory.

exercises: Create an open Shape class with an open area() method
Create Circle and Rectangle subclasses that override area()
Create a Drawable interface and implement it in multiple classes

quiz.question: What keyword is required for overriding?
Can a class implement multiple interfaces?

modules.title: Data Classes & Sealed Classes
objectives: Use data classes for model objects

content.value: Data classes auto-generate equals(), hashCode(), toString(), copy(), and componentN() functions. Sealed classes define restricted class hierarchies where all subclasses are known at compile time.

Data classes are like prefab houses - you get plumbing, wiring, and doors auto-installed. Sealed classes are like a fixed menu at a restaurant - you know exactly what options exist, and the kitchen is prepared for all of them.

content.title: Data Classes & Sealed Classes
content.code:
data class User(val id: Int, val name: String, val email: String)

val user1 = User(1, "Alice", "alice@example.com")
val user2 = User(1, "Alice", "alice@example.com")
println(user1 == user2)  // true
println(user1.copy(email = "new@example.com"))

val (id, name) = user1
println("$id: $name")

sealed class Result {
    data class Success(val data: String) : Result()
    data class Error(val msg: String) : Result()
    object Loading : Result()
}

fun handle(result: Result) = when (result) {
    is Result.Success -> println(result.data)
    is Result.Error -> println(result.msg)
    Result.Loading -> println("Loading...")
}

content.value: Data classes must have at least one primary constructor parameter. Sealed classes are abstract by default. The when expression on sealed classes is exhaustive - the compiler ensures all cases are handled. Use object for singleton subclasses.

exercises: Create a data class for a Book with title, author, year
Use copy() to create a modified version of a book
Create a sealed class for network states (Success, Error, Loading)

modules.title: Collections & Functional API

content.value: Kotlin collections are divided into mutable and read-only interfaces. The standard library provides rich functional APIs: filter, map, flatMap, reduce, fold, groupBy, and more. Sequences enable lazy evaluation for performance.

Functional collection operations are like an assembly line. filter() removes defective items, map() transforms each piece, reduce() combines everything into one. Sequences are like a lazy conveyor belt that only processes items when needed.

content.title: Collections & Functional API

content.value: Prefer immutable collections (listOf, setOf) over mutable. Use sequences when chaining multiple operations on large collections to avoid intermediate lists. Kotlin collections are Java collections under the hood with enhanced APIs.

exercises: Given a list of numbers, filter even numbers, double them, and sum the result
Group a list of strings by their length
Use flatMap to flatten a list of lists and apply a transformation

modules.title: Extension Functions & Properties
objectives: Write extension functions on existing types
Use scope functions (let, apply, run, with, also)

content.value: Extension functions add new behavior to existing classes without inheritance. Scope functions (let, apply, run, with, also) provide a context object within a lambda. They are essential for concise Kotlin code.

Extension functions are like being allowed to add a new gear to someone else's car. The car manufacturer (class author) doesn't need to know - your extension just works. Scope functions are like a focused workbench where everything revolves around one tool.

content.title: Extensions & Scope Functions

explanation.desc: Operate on object, returns lambda result

content.value: Extension functions are statically dispatched - they are resolved at compile time based on the declared type, not runtime type. Scope functions differ primarily by how they provide context (this vs it) and what they return (receiver vs lambda result).

exercises: Add an extension function to String that capitalizes every word
Use apply to initialize a complex object with multiple properties
Use let with safe calls to operate on nullable values

quiz.question: Which scope function returns the receiver?

modules.title: Generics & Delegation

content.value: Generics enable type-safe abstractions. Kotlin supports declaration-site variance (in/out) instead of Java's use-site wildcards. Reified generics preserve type information at runtime. Delegation provides a lightweight alternative to inheritance.

Generics are like a universal remote that works with any TV brand (type). Variance (in/out) is like specifying whether the remote can send (out), receive (in), or both. Delegation is like hiring a personal assistant (by) to handle your calls.

content.title: Generics & Delegation

content.value: out (covariant) means the class can only produce T. in (contravariant) means it can only consume T. Use reified in inline functions to access the type at runtime. The by keyword delegates interface implementation to a composed object.

exercises: Create a generic Stack<T> class with push, pop, peek methods
Use reified generics to create a type-safe JSON parser function
Use delegation to implement a logging wrapper around a repository interface

modules.title: Coroutines & Concurrency

content.value: Coroutines are lightweight threads for asynchronous programming. launch fires and forgets, async returns a Deferred (future). Suspend functions are the building blocks of coroutines - they can be paused and resumed without blocking threads.

Coroutines are like a juggler who can pause mid-throw, answer the phone, then resume juggling exactly where they left off. Compare to threads - having multiple jugglers (expensive). Coroutines are like a single juggler who is just really good at multitasking.

content.title: Coroutines

content.value: runBlocking is for main functions and tests. Use viewModelScope, lifecycleScope, or custom scopes in production. Structured concurrency ensures all child coroutines complete before the parent completes. Flows are cold - they start when collected.

exercises: Write a program that fetches two simulated API responses concurrently using async/await
Create a flow that emits numbers from 1 to 5 with delays
Handle cancellation in a coroutine using isActive check

modules.title: DSLs & Type Safety

content.value: Kotlin DSLs leverage lambda with receiver, extension functions, and @DslMarker to create type-safe, readable domain-specific languages. This pattern is used in libraries like kotlinx.html, Ktor, and Gradle Kotlin DSL.

A DSL is like creating a custom language for a specific job. Instead of writing regular code, you write in a language that reads like the problem domain. Lambda with receiver is like giving a tour guide (receiver) who shows you around while you give instructions.

content.title: Type-Safe DSL

explanation.desc: Restricts implicit receiver access to prevent ambiguity
Build tree structure from DSL calls

content.value: @DslMarker prevents accessing outer receiver members when an inner receiver is available. This avoids confusion when multiple receivers are in scope. Lambda with receiver (Receiver.() -> Unit) allows using members of the receiver directly.

exercises: Create a small HTML DSL that supports div and span elements
Build a CSS property DSL with nested selectors
Apply @DslMarker to prevent receiver leakage

modules.title: Testing with Kotlin

content.value: Kotlin testing combines JUnit 5 with Kotlin-specific frameworks. Kotest provides kotest assertions, property-based testing, and behavior specs. MockK is a Kotlin-first mocking library that handles coroutines, sealed classes, and extension functions.

Testing in Kotlin is like having a quality inspector for your code. JUnit 5 is the inspection checklist, Kotest adds specialized tools for Kotlin parts, and MockK creates realistic test dummies that behave like the real components.

content.title: Testing with Kotlin

content.value: Use backtick test names with spaces for readability. Kotest matchers (shouldBe, shouldContain, shouldThrow) provide fluent assertions. MockK handles coroutine mocking with coEvery/coVerify. Use @BeforeEach or @TestInstance for test lifecycle.

exercises: Write a JUnit 5 test for a function that reverses a string
Use Kotest matchers to test list operations
Create a MockK test that mocks a repository and verifies interactions
